worth_it_explanation: Child-directed treatment decides whether inventory that reaches children carries the right regulatory signal before it goes to buyers. A site or app that should be flagged but is not exposes the publisher to a COPPA violation, and the penalties are set per affected child, so the exposure scales with traffic. The check is a review of how the publisher classifies its inventory in Magnite and what signal Magnite passes downstream, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete classification decision the publisher can correct.
intro: The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) governs websites and online services directed to children under 13 years of age in the United States. For a publisher monetizing through Magnite, the audit question is whether inventory that reaches children is classified as child-directed and whether that classification reaches buyers through the bid request, so personalized ads and certain data uses are suppressed on it. This subcategory covers two checks. The first confirms that child-directed inventory is flagged at the source so the signal travels with the impression. The second confirms that restricted data processing actually applies to that inventory, rather than the flag being set while identifiers and precise data still flow.

audit_checks.title: Child-directed inventory is not flagged at the source
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Inventory that reaches children but carries no child-directed flag sends a personalized-advertising bid request when it should send a suppressed one, which is a direct Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) exposure. COPPA penalties are assessed per affected child, so a single misclassified site or app that draws meaningful child traffic can produce liability that scales with reach, which makes an unflagged child-directed property a catastrophic gap rather than a hygiene issue.

educate.base:
COPPA imposes requirements on operators of websites and online services directed to children under 13 years of age in the United States. For a publisher selling inventory through Magnite, the practical effect is that any property directed to children, or a section of a property that reaches them, has to be flagged as child-directed so the demand side knows to suppress personalized advertising and limit the data it collects.

Magnite carries this signal in the bid request. A seller designates inventory as directed to children, and Magnite sets 'regs.coppa' on the OpenRTB request to note that the impression falls within COPPA's scope. That flag is how a buyer learns to drop personalization and treat the impression under children's-privacy rules. When the flag is missing, the impression looks like ordinary inventory, and a buyer can bid with personalized targeting on an audience the law protects.

The signal only works if the classification is correct at the source. Magnite passes downstream what the seller declares, so an unflagged child-directed site or app produces a bid request that says personalization is permitted when it is not. The fix is a classification decision the publisher controls, not a Magnite setting, which is why the audit looks at how the publisher decides which inventory is child-directed and whether that decision reaches the platform.

A few signs point to child-directed inventory that is not flagged:
  • A site, app, or section that targets or knowingly reaches children under 13 has no child-directed designation in Magnite
  • The bid request for that inventory carries no COPPA signal, so 'regs.coppa' is absent or set to 0
  • The publisher cannot say which properties were reviewed for child-directed status or when that review last happened
You may want to confirm that every property reaching children under 13 is designated as child-directed in Magnite, and that the designation is reviewed when new sites, apps, or sections are added. One approach is to inventory the properties by audience, mark the ones directed to children, and check that the COPPA signal appears on their bid requests.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a kids' section, an education vertical, or a games area can reach children under 13 even when the main site is general-audience. You may want to review each section separately rather than classifying the whole domain as one, so a child-directed area carries the flag while the general-audience areas are not over-restricted.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a toys category, a children's-products area, or family-oriented gift content can draw an audience that includes children under 13. You may want to confirm whether those areas are directed to children under COPPA's standard, and flag them as child-directed in Magnite if they are, so the commercial inventory aimed at kids does not run personalized advertising.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed guidance on designating child-directed inventory in the platform is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review how the publisher classifies inventory as child-directed and confirm that the COPPA signal appears on the bid requests for that inventory.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Open the inventory or property settings in the Magnite console (the SpringServe or Demand Manager UI, depending on the integration) and list the active sites, apps, and sections.
For each property, note whether a child-directed or COPPA designation is set, and record which properties carry it.
Compare that list against the properties the publisher knows to target or reach children under 13, and flag any property that reaches children but carries no child-directed designation.
Ask the publisher when child-directed status was last reviewed and whether new properties are checked at launch, and record the answer.

bid_request_inspection.label: Bid request inspection
steps: On a property the publisher believes is child-directed, capture a sample bid request (e.g., through the browser network panel for a web placement or a request log for an app placement).
Confirm whether 'regs.coppa' is present and set to 1 on that request, which signals COPPA scope to buyers.
Record any property whose request carries no COPPA signal despite reaching children, which is the impression a buyer would treat as ordinary inventory.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per property, whether it is directed to or knowingly reaches children under 13, whether it carries a child-directed designation in Magnite, and whether its bid request sets the COPPA signal. A setup where every property reaching children is designated child-directed and its requests carry the signal passes. A property that reaches children under 13 but carries no designation, or whose requests carry no COPPA signal, is a fail recorded with the property and the missing signal.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each property's child audience, its child-directed designation in Magnite, and whether its bid request sets the COPPA signal above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has inventory reaching children under 13 that carries no child-directed designation in Magnite, so its bid requests do not set the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) signal that tells buyers to suppress personalized advertising. Magnite passes downstream what the seller declares, so an unflagged child-directed property produces a request that permits personalization on an audience the law protects, and COPPA penalties are assessed per affected child. {{voice_recommend}} inventorying the properties by audience, designating each one that reaches children under 13 as child-directed in Magnite, and confirming that 'regs.coppa' appears on its bid requests. After the change, review child-directed status whenever a new site, app, or section is added so the classification stays current.

audit_checks.title: Restricted data processing is not applied to child-directed inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A child-directed flag that is set while identifiers, precise location, and personalized targeting still flow gives the publisher a false sense of compliance. The signal says the impression is protected, but the data accompanying it is not restricted, so the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) exposure remains while looking resolved, which makes the gap serious even though the flag itself is present.

educate.base:
Flagging inventory as child-directed is the first step under COPPA, and restricting the data that travels with it is the second. The flag tells buyers that COPPA applies. The data restriction is what actually keeps the impression compliant, by stripping the identifiers and precise signals that personalized advertising depends on.

When the COPPA signal is set, the supply chain anonymizes the bid request before it reaches buyers. In a header-bidding setup that runs through Prebid, for example, setting the COPPA flag causes the request to drop device identifiers such as the advertising ID and hashed device IDs, truncate the IP address, and remove precise geolocation and user-level data. Magnite applies its own restrictions on inventory a seller designates as child-directed, limiting the data it collects and the data buyers can access on that inventory. The result is non-personalized advertising on an audience the law protects.

The gap this check looks for is a flag set in name only. If the inventory carries the child-directed designation but identifiers, precise location, or personalized targeting still flow on its requests, the restriction is not taking effect. That can happen when a header-bidding wrapper passes the COPPA signal to some adapters but not all, when a direct integration sets the flag without enabling the matching data controls, or when a user-ID module continues to attach an identifier the COPPA path should have removed.

A few signs point to restricted processing that is not applied:
  • Inventory is designated child-directed, but its bid requests still carry a device or user identifier
  • Precise geolocation or a full IP address appears on requests that should be truncated under COPPA
  • A user-ID or audience module is active on child-directed inventory and continues to attach an identifier to the request
You may want to confirm that the data restriction matches the flag on every piece of child-directed inventory. One approach is to capture a sample bid request from child-directed inventory and check that identifiers, precise location, and personalized signals have been removed rather than relying on the flag alone.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a child-directed section may share a header-bidding wrapper and user-ID setup with the general-audience sections. You may want to confirm that the COPPA path suppresses the identity modules on the child-directed section specifically, so an identifier meant for general-audience inventory does not attach to a child-directed request.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, child-directed product areas may run the same audience and remarketing setup as the rest of the catalog. You may want to confirm that remarketing identifiers and audience signals are removed on the child-directed inventory, which are the personalized signals COPPA restricts even when the flag is present.

learn_more.source_title: Privacy for Header Bidding, COPPA anonymization

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's documentation on the data controls it applies to child-directed inventory is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether the data restriction matches the child-directed flag by inspecting what actually travels on the bid requests for that inventory.

bid_request_inspection.label: Bid request inspection
steps: On a piece of inventory designated child-directed, capture a sample bid request through the browser network panel or a request log.
Confirm 'regs.coppa' is set to 1, then check the request for device identifiers (e.g., the advertising ID or a hashed device ID), a full IP address, and precise geolocation.
Record any identifier, full IP, or precise location that still appears, which is a signal the COPPA restriction should have removed.

wrapper_config_review.label: Header-bidding wrapper config review
steps: If the inventory runs through a Prebid wrapper, review the config to confirm the COPPA setting is enabled and passed to every adapter, not a subset.
Check whether any user-ID or audience module is active on the child-directed inventory and whether the COPPA path suppresses it.
Record any adapter or module that receives the request without the COPPA restriction applied.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for child-directed inventory, whether the bid requests have been stripped of device identifiers, precise location, and personalized signals to match the COPPA flag. Inventory where the flag is set and the request carries no identifier, truncated IP, and no precise location passes. Inventory designated child-directed whose requests still carry an identifier, a full IP, precise geolocation, or an active personalized module is a fail recorded with the signal that leaked and where.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record for each child-directed property whether the bid request has been stripped of identifiers, precise location, and personalized signals above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} flags inventory as child-directed but still passes identifiers, precise location, or personalized signals on its bid requests, so the data restriction the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) requires is not taking effect. The flag tells buyers COPPA applies, but the request still carries the data personalized advertising depends on, which leaves the exposure in place while looking resolved. {{voice_recommend}} capturing a sample bid request from the child-directed inventory, confirming that device identifiers, a full IP address, and precise geolocation have been removed, and checking that the COPPA setting is passed to every adapter and that user-ID modules are suppressed on that inventory. After the change, recheck a sample request to confirm the data restriction matches the flag.
